title: Agentic Harness Engineering: Observability-Driven Automatic Evolution of Coding-Agent Harnesses
authors: Jiahang Lin, Shichun Liu, Chengjun Pan, Lizhi Lin, Shihan Dou, Zhiheng Xi, Xuanjing Huang, Hang Yan, Zhenhua Han, Tao Gui, Yu-Gang Jiang
problem: Harness engineering (prompt/tools/middleware) is a manual craft; automate the improvement of coding-agent harnesses.
unit_of_analysis: Editable harness components of a single coding agent on one benchmark.
intervention: Automatic harness-evolution loop that proposes and applies component edits.
method: Three observability mechanisms: component (explicit editable elements), experience (distill trajectories to evidence), decision (pair each edit with a prediction).
main_claim: Ten AHE iterations lift pass@1 from 69.7% to 77.0%, beating human-designed baselines; evolved harness transfers without re-evolution.
relation_to_ours: VERIFIED ANCHOR / direct parent. Does the automatic editing we explicitly do NOT claim, and its 'decision observability' is exactly the edit self-prediction we also exclude. It does NOT predict which tasks regress before a full suite, does NOT select diverse canaries at a fixed budget, has no calibrated abstain/escalate, and no full regression ground truth or held-out generalization. Our work is the downstream regression-foresight layer for the edits AHE-style systems produce.

title: Self-Harness: Harnesses That Improve Themselves
authors: Hangfan Zhang, Shao Zhang, Kangcong Li, Chen Zhang, Yang Chen, Yiqun Zhang, Lei Bai, Shuyue Hu
problem: Effective harness design is model-specific yet hand-built; let an agent evolve its own harness per base model.
unit_of_analysis: Harness edits proposed per base model from execution traces.
intervention: Iterative self-evolution loop (weakness mining -> minimal harness proposal -> validation).
method: Trace-based weakness mining, diverse minimal candidate edits, and a regression-testing gate that promotes only edits passing tests.
main_claim: Large gains (up to ~21pp absolute); critically, the agent's own cross-iteration regression prediction is near-random (~2x baseline) -- agents can justify why an edit should help but cannot name the tasks it will break.
relation_to_ours: HIGHEST collision. Establishes our exact motivation (hidden regressions are real; naive self-prediction fails) and already uses regression testing as an accept gate. BUT it runs ad-hoc/full regression tests, not an edit-conditioned diverse canary SELECTION that maximizes regression recall at a fixed k<<|D| budget; no calibrated abstention/escalation policy; no held-out generalization across edit type/task family/model family. Our delta is precisely the selection + risk-control layer it lacks.

title: Harness Updating Is Not Harness Benefit: Disentangling Evolution Capabilities in Self-Evolving LLM Agents
problem: Does base model capability predict ability to generate vs. benefit from self-evolved harnesses (prompts/skills/memory/tools)?
unit_of_analysis: Two capabilities (harness-updating vs harness-benefit) across model capability tiers.
intervention: Comparative analysis of how models of different sizes generate and apply harness updates.
method: Controlled comparison across capability tiers measuring updating vs benefit.
data: Self-evolving agent benchmarks with prompt/skill/memory/tool edits.
main_claim: Harness-updating ability is flat in base capability while harness-benefit is non-monotonic (mid-tier models benefit most); invest in the task-solving agent over the evolution mechanism.
relation_to_ours: Same self-evolving-harness family but orthogonal question: who benefits from edits, not whether edits cause hidden regressions or how to foresee them cheaply. Supports our premise that edit effects are heterogeneous/model-dependent (motivating model-family held-out). No canary selection, prediction, or abstention.

title: When Generic Prompt Improvements Hurt: Evaluation-Driven Iteration for LLM Applications
problem: Generic prompt edits do not improve uniformly and can silently regress LLM applications; need evaluation before deployment.
unit_of_analysis: Prompt variants tested against task-specific evaluation suites.
intervention: Five prompt conditions (incl. appended generic rules) compared per application category.
method: Minimum Viable Evaluation Suite (MVES) linking categories to failure modes/metrics, plus a reproducible local eval harness running ~30-case-per-suite ablations.
data: Llama 3 8B and Qwen 2.5 7B on structured extraction, RAG, and instruction-following.
main_claim: Generic prompt additions are non-monotonic; e.g., Qwen 2.5 on RAG drops from 26/30 to 9/30 when generic rules are appended -- regressions must be caught by evaluation-driven iteration.
relation_to_ours: Strong motivation overlap for one component (prompt edits cause per-task hidden regressions). BUT it only characterizes and runs the full task-specific suite; it does NOT predict which tasks regress before the suite, does NOT select canaries at a fixed budget, and has no abstain/escalate. Single component, fixed small suites -- not edit-conditioned selection across harness component types.

title: VeRO: A Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
authors: Varun Ursekar, Apaar Shanker, Veronica Chatrath, Yuan Xue, Samuel Marc Denton
problem: Systematically optimize agent prompts/configs rather than manual tuning.
unit_of_analysis: Agent prompt/configuration optimization for a target agent.
intervention: Meta-agents propose edits to a target agent's prompt/config and evaluate on validation tasks.
method: Evaluation harness: run target agent, capture failure traces, generate refinements, validate, iterate on performance deltas (incl. regression analysis on validation set).
data: SWE-Bench, HumanEval, and domain task collections.
main_claim: Programmatic agent-optimizing-agent beats ad-hoc manual prompt engineering across benchmarks.
relation_to_ours: Another automatic harness/agent optimizer (the thing our novelty boundary excludes). It does validation-set regression analysis as a side-check but not edit-conditioned diverse canary selection, fixed-budget regression recall, calibrated abstention/escalation, or held-out generalization. Upstream optimizer, not foresight method.

title: 100 instances is all you need: predicting the success of a new LLM on unseen data by testing on a few instances
authors: Lorenzo Pacchiardi, Lucy G. Cheke, Jose Hernandez-Orallo
venue_or_status: KDD workshop on Evaluation and Trustworthiness of Generative AI Models
problem: Predict per-instance success of a new LLM on unseen data without exhaustively testing it.
intervention: Test a new model on a small reference set (~100 instances), then predict the rest.
method: Generic assessor predicting instance success from reference-instance performance plus instance features.
data: HELM-Lite, KindsOfReasoning; OpenAI instruction-tuned models through GPT-4.
main_claim: ~100 instances suffice to match LLM-specific assessors; random reference selection performs about as well as advanced selection in-distribution; degrades out-of-distribution.
relation_to_ours: Close on mechanism (predict per-instance outcome of a new system from a few tested instances) and a natural baseline. BUT it predicts absolute success of a model, not the edit-induced pass->fail REGRESSION delta between old/new harness; it is not edit-conditioned; it finds random selection sufficient (the opposite of our H2 that selection matters under structured regressions); no abstention/escalation; not agent harnesses.

title: Active Evaluation Acquisition for Efficient LLM Benchmarking
authors: Yang Li, Jie Ma, Miguel Ballesteros, Yassine Benajiba, Graham Horwood
problem: Comprehensive benchmarking over many prompts is costly; evaluate a subset and infer the rest.
unit_of_analysis: Individual evaluation examples within large benchmarks.
intervention: Actively select which examples to evaluate, then predict outcomes on the unevaluated remainder.
method: RL-based learned acquisition policy modeling cross-example dependencies to forecast unevaluated outcomes.
data: Diverse benchmark datasets across LLM capabilities.
main_claim: A learned selection policy significantly reduces the number of evaluation prompts while maintaining accurate performance estimates.
relation_to_ours: Closest method neighbor on 'learn to select a few items + predict the rest' and a strong baseline. BUT the objective is aggregate performance estimation for ONE model, not edit-conditioned per-task regression detection between two harnesses; selection is not conditioned on a structured diff; no abstain/escalate; not agent harnesses.

title: Rapid Regression Detection in Software Deployments through Sequential Testing
authors: Michael Lindon, Chris Sanden, Vache Shirikian
problem: Detect regressions quickly during canary deployments while controlling false detections.
intervention: Sequential (always-valid) hypothesis testing on stochastic order and distributional equality.
method: Anytime-valid sequential tests enabling continuous monitoring with strict false-positive control.
data: Two industrial case studies at Netflix.
main_claim: Sequential testing detects deployment regressions rapidly while strictly controlling the false-detection probability throughout monitoring.
relation_to_ours: Provides the sequential/escalation statistical machinery behind our 'escalate when uncertain' module and a 'canary' framing. BUT it monitors aggregate metrics of canary TRAFFIC; it does not SELECT which tasks/canaries to run, is not edit-conditioned, and is not about agent task suites. Method neighbor for the sequential-decision layer only.

title: Conformal Selective Prediction with General Risk Control (SCoRE)
problem: Decide when a model can be trusted vs should abstain, with strict finite-sample risk control over trusted cases.
unit_of_analysis: Per-instance trust/abstention decisions for any trained model.
intervention: Calibrated abstention: trust only when a risk guarantee holds.
method: SCoRE constructs generalized e-values feeding hypothesis tests for trust/abstain decisions with finite-sample, exchangeability-based guarantees, extending to distribution shift.
data: Methodological; applications in drug discovery, health risk, LLMs.
main_claim: Provides distribution-free finite-sample error control over trusted cases without uniform concentration, and extends to distribution-shift.
relation_to_ours: Method neighbor for our abstain/escalate guarantees (conformal risk control / e-values). Not applied to agents, harness edits, regression detection, or canary selection. We adopt/compare against this family for the risk-controlled decision, but the agent-harness regression-foresight problem and the selection objective are ours.

title: Positive-Congruent Training: Towards Regression-Free Model Updates
authors: Sijie Yan, Yuanjun Xiong, Kaustav Kundu, Shuo Yang, Siqi Deng, Meng Wang, Wei Xia, Stefano Soatto
problem: Updated models introduce 'negative flips' -- regressions on examples the old model got right.
intervention: Modify training to reduce negative flips while improving accuracy.
method: Focal Distillation upweighting samples the reference model classified correctly; ensemble reference for further reduction.
main_claim: Reduces model regression (negative-flip rate) while preserving accuracy gains.
relation_to_ours: Canonical definition of the regression we study (pass->fail / negative flips), but for MODEL updates via training, not harness edits. It PREVENTS regressions by retraining; it does not PREDICT which tasks regress, select canaries, or abstain. Different unit (weights vs harness component edits) and different goal (mitigation vs cheap foresight).

title: Test Case Selection and Prioritization Using Machine Learning: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Rongqi Pan, Mojtaba Bagherzadeh, Taher A. Ghaleb, Lionel Briand
problem: Running all tests in CI is costly; select/prioritize tests for fast regression feedback.
unit_of_analysis: Test cases in CI pipelines (29 primary studies, 2006-2020).
intervention: ML-based test case selection and prioritization (TSP).
method: Survey/taxonomy of ML-TSP techniques, features (code change/history signals), metrics, and reproducibility.
main_claim: ML combines partial/imperfect test-case signals into accurate prediction models for regression testing; provides a TSP taxonomy.
relation_to_ours: Defines the classic 'this is just regression test selection' attack we must rebut. Deterministic software, code-diff features, single system. Our setting differs: stochastic LLM agents, edit-semantics features over harness components, model x harness interactions, full-suite regression ground truth, calibrated abstention, and held-out edit/task/model generalization. We must implement the strongest reproducible TSP baseline.

title: Beyond the Mean: Within-Model Reliable Change Detection for LLM Evaluation
problem: Distinguish genuine per-item change between model versions from stochastic sampling noise in LLM evaluation.
unit_of_analysis: Individual benchmark items (2,000 MMLU-Pro items, 10 samples each at T=0.7).
intervention: Item-level reliable-change assessment between two model versions.
method: Adapts the Reliable Change Index (clinical psychology) to item-level shifts.
data: MMLU-Pro; Llama 3->3.1 and Qwen 2.5->3 within-family comparisons.
main_claim: Despite small aggregate gains, large bidirectional item-level churn; greedy single-shot eval missed 42% of reliably changed items and falsely flagged 25% of unchanged items.
relation_to_ours: Directly supports our regression-label definition under stochasticity (per-item churn is large and aggregate scores hide it) and the need to control measurement noise. BUT it only detects change after full sampling on all items; no selection, no edit conditioning, no abstention, and it concerns model upgrades not harness edits or agents.
